98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean
2	
3	\chapter{The invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of a Weil divisor (RR.2\(_\ast\))}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OcOfD}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II~\S6, pp.~144--145
7	% (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\); Proposition 6.13; Remark 6.17.1;
8	% Proposition 6.18) and IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive-step short exact
9	% sequence). Read directly from
10	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162 and
11	% 313, by rendering the scanned page images and transcribing the
12	% statements character-by-character (the PDF has no text layer). Stacks
13	% pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (Weil divisors), 0AUW
14	% (the sheaf \(\mathcal O_X(D)\) from a Weil divisor), 0BE3 (degree-zero of
15	% a principal divisor on a complete nonsingular curve).
16	
17	\section{Setup and motivation}
18	
19	This chapter is a satellite of \texttt{RR.2}
20	(REF); together with \texttt{RR.1}
21	(REF), \texttt{RR.3}
22	(REF), and \texttt{RR.4}
23	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex}) it forms the four-stage
24	sub-build closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
25	\[
26	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
27	  \,\colon\,
28	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
29	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
30	\]
31	The Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain consumed by REF
32	of \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} relies on the invertible-sheaf
33	functor
34	\[
35	  \mathcal O_C(-) \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}(C) \;\longrightarrow\;
36	  \mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})
37	\]
38	sending a Weil divisor \(D = \sum_i n_i [P_i]\) to its associated invertible
39	sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) on \(C\). The Lean entry-point
40	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} of
41	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} (line~168) currently
42	encodes this functor as a typed-\texttt{sorry} \texttt{noncomputable def}
43	with no chapter pinning. This chapter supplies the missing pinning by
44	blueprinting the Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}
45	(to be created in iter-183), where the body of \texttt{sheafOf} and its
46	three immediate corollaries will live.
47	
48	\paragraph{Why this chapter is gated to \texttt{RR.2}.} The
49	\(\chi\)-identity proof of REF
50	in \texttt{RR.2} is an induction on the Weil divisor \(D\) via the
51	``\(D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]\)'' step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3. That step
52	intrinsically references the body of \texttt{sheafOf}: it asks for the
53	short exact sequence
54	\[
55	  0 \to \mathcal O_C(D) \to \mathcal O_C(D + [Y]) \to k(Y)^{[\text{at } Y]}
56	  \to 0
57	\]
58	to be exact in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\), which is not a statement on the
59	\emph{signature} of \texttt{sheafOf} but on its \emph{body}. The iter-181
60	Lane H factoring of \texttt{RR.2} consequently
61	landed two helper sorries
62	(\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_zero},
63	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_succ}) that are
64	\emph{intrinsically gated} on the body of \texttt{sheafOf}. Until this
65	chapter pins the body, those helpers remain vacuous regardless of how
66	much \(\chi\)-machinery \texttt{RR.2} adds on top.
67	
68	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.3}.} The
69	existing \texttt{RR.3} chapter (REF) constructs
70	the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) associated to a \emph{closed point}
71	\(P \in C\) directly, bypassing the Weil-divisor packaging. The
72	REF below makes this bypass mathematically
73	coherent by exhibiting \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) at \(D = [P]\) as canonically
74	isomorphic to REF. With this isomorphism in
75	hand, \texttt{RR.3}'s downstream consumption of
76	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via the
77	\(\chi\)-identity routes through \texttt{sheafOf} cleanly.
78	
79	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.4}.} The
80	two pins consumed by the \texttt{RR.4} chapter
81	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex})
82	that translate Riemann--Roch outputs into the function-field/degree
83	language of \(C\) both reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) for general \(D\) (not
84	just \(D = [P]\)): the function-field-degree bridge takes a divisor
85	\(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) and reads off \(\deg(D)\) from the
86	\texttt{sheafOf}-associated invertible sheaf, while the
87	Weil-divisor / line-bundle correspondence is the substance of
88	Hartshorne's Proposition~6.15 (which the present chapter pins via
89	REF).
90	
91	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
92	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
93	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
94	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}) carry the same
95	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
96	\begin{quote}
97	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
98	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
99	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
100	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
101	\end{quote}
102	The Weil divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) is the one defined in
103	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex}; the
104	prime-divisor data type is REF; the order
105	\(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor \(Q\) is REF; the
106	principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a non-zero rational function is
107	REF; the closed-point divisor \([P]\) is
108	REF. The line bundle at a closed point
109	already constructed in \texttt{RR.3} is
110	REF, and the present chapter's
111	REF makes the comparison
112	\(\mathcal O_C([P]) = \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}\) explicit.
113	
114	\paragraph{Route alignment with iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom}
115	analogy.} The iter-182 api-alignment analogy
116	\texttt{analogies/ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md} surveyed three candidate
117	Mathlib paths for the closed-point specialisation \(\mathcal O_C([P])\):
118	(i)~\texttt{Sheaf-internal-Hom} on \(\mathbf{SheafOfModules}\) (rejected:
119	no \texttt{IdealSheafData \(\to\) SheafOfModules} realiser in Mathlib,
120	no \(\mathbf{SheafOfModules}\) dual functor in Mathlib);
121	(ii)~the abstract dual $\mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
122	\mathcal O_C)$ in a new \texttt{IdealSheafDual.lean} (rejected: high
123	LOC cost in exchange for a single sheaf the project can build directly);
124	(iii)~Hartshorne's \emph{subsheaf-of-\(\mathcal K_C\)} description (accepted).
125	The present chapter adopts route~(iii) for the general
126	\texttt{sheafOf \(D\)} as well, and REF verifies
127	that the general construction at \(D = [P]\) recovers the closed-point
128	specialisation of \texttt{RR.3} on the nose. The subsheaf-of-\(\mathcal K_C\)
129	recipe is mathematically Hartshorne's original (II~\S6, the definition of
130	\(\mathscr L(D)\)) and was equally available for the closed-point case; the
131	project's choice to use it both at the general \(D\) and at the \(D = [P]\)
132	specialisation is what makes the comparison
133	\(\mathcal O_C([P]) \cong \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}\) a direct unfolding
134	rather than a non-trivial isomorphism.
135	
136	\section{The invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)}
137	
138	\begin{definition}\leanok
139	[Invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of a Weil divisor]
140	  \label{def:sheafOf}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf}
142	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:prime_divisor, def:order_at_point,
143	        def:Scheme_toModuleKPresheaf, thm:Scheme_toModuleKPresheaf_isSheaf}
144	
145	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
146	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor on a scheme),
147	  % and Remark~6.17.1, Proposition~6.18 + Proposition~6.15, p.~145
148	  % (Cartier \(\leftrightarrow\) Weil correspondence on an integral
149	  % locally factorial scheme; identification of \(\mathscr L(D)\) as a
150	  % subsheaf of the constant sheaf at the function field \(K\)). Read from
151	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162,
152	  % transcribed character-by-character from rendered scanned page
153	  % images (the PDF has no text layer).
154	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
155	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
156	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
157	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
158	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
159	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), so \(f_i^{-1}\) and
160	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
161	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
162	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.15. \emph{If \(X\) is an integral
163	  % scheme, the homomorphism \(\mathrm{CaCl}\, X \to \mathrm{Pic}\, X\) of
164	  % (6.14) is an isomorphism.} Proof. We have only to show that every
165	  % invertible sheaf is isomorphic to a subsheaf of \(\mathscr K\), which
166	  % in this case is the constant sheaf \(K\), where \(K\) is the function
167	  % field of \(X\)."
168	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Remark 6.17.1. Clearly this gives a 1-1
169	  % correspondence between effective Cartier divisors on \(X\) and
170	  % \emph{locally principal} closed subschemes \(Y\), i.e., subschemes
171	  % whose sheaf of ideals is locally generated by a single element.
172	  % Note also that if \(X\) is an integral separated noetherian locally
173	  % factorial scheme, so that the Cartier divisors correspond to Weil
174	  % divisors by (6.11), then the effective Cartier divisors correspond
175	  % exactly to the effective Weil divisors."
176	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (the definition of
177	  \(\mathscr L(D)\)), Proposition~6.15 (the constant-sheaf-at-\(K\)
178	  identification on an integral scheme), and Remark~6.17.1 (Weil
179	  divisors and Cartier divisors agree on an integral separated
180	  noetherian locally factorial scheme).}
181	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
182	  \(\bar k\) and let \(D = \sum_{Q} n_Q [Q] \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor
183	  on \(C\), with the formal sum indexed by prime divisors \(Q\) of \(C\) and
184	  supported on a finite set \(\{Q : n_Q \neq 0\}\)
185	  (REF). The \emph{invertible sheaf
186	  \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\)}, also written
187	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) := \mathscr L(D)\) in Hartshorne's notation, is the
188	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the constant sheaf
189	  \(\mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\) (Proposition~6.15: on an integral scheme the
190	  sheaf of total quotient rings is the constant sheaf at the function
191	  field) defined section-wise by
192	  \[
193	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(D)\bigr) \;:=\;
194	    \{\, 0 \,\} \;\cup\;
195	    \bigl\{\, f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\;
196	      \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; -\,n_Q \text{ for every prime divisor }
197	      Q \text{ of } C \text{ with } Q \in U \bigr\}
198	  \]
199	  for each open \(U \subseteq C\). Restriction along \(V \hookrightarrow U\)
200	  is the identity on the underlying carrier \(K(C)\), which preserves
201	  membership (a smaller open imposes \emph{fewer} order constraints, so
202	  the constraint set on \(U\) embeds into the constraint set on \(V\)). The
203	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on
204	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) is restricted from the
205	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on \(K(C)\) (a \(\bar k\)-scalar multiple of a
206	  rational function has the same order at every prime divisor).
207	
208	  \paragraph{Equivalence with the Cartier-divisor description.} The
209	  formal sum \(D = \sum_Q n_Q [Q]\) packages, on a curve where prime
210	  divisors are exactly closed points and the local rings at prime
211	  divisors are DVRs (smoothness \(+\) dimension~\(1\)), the Cartier-divisor
212	  data \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) of Hartshorne's definition: locally on a small
213	  open \(U_i\) containing exactly one prime divisor \(Q\) in the support of
214	  \(D\), pick a uniformiser \(\pi_Q \in K(C)\) at \(Q\) and set
215	  \(f_i := \pi_Q^{n_Q}\); on opens disjoint from the support of \(D\), set
216	  \(f_i := 1\). The transitions on overlaps \(U_i \cap U_j\) are units in
217	  \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}^{\times}\) for each prime \(Q\) in the overlap (any
218	  two uniformisers differ by a unit, by the DVR structure), so the
219	  \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) define a Cartier divisor whose Weil-divisor image
220	  under Hartshorne's (6.11) is the original \(D\). The
221	  ``\(f_i^{-1}\)-generated'' subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\) from Hartshorne's
222	  definition then coincides with the section-wise description above:
223	  the local generator \(f_i^{-1} = \pi_Q^{-n_Q}\) has order \(-n_Q\) at \(Q\),
224	  so the local sections are exactly \(f \in K(C)\) with
225	  \(\ord_Q(f \cdot \pi_Q^{n_Q}) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q\), on
226	  \(U_i\).
227	
228	  Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) (quoted in
229	  REF of REF)
230	  then guarantees that the resulting subsheaf $\mathcal O_C(D) \subset
231	  \mathcal K_C$ is an invertible sheaf on $C$, independently of the
232	  choice of uniformisers \(\pi_Q\) (any two choices differ by a unit, so
233	  they generate the same \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule).
234	
235	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
236	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} takes a Weil
237	  divisor \(D : C.\text{left.WeilDivisor}\) and returns a
238	  \(\texttt{Sheaf}~J~(\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\) on
239	  \(C.\text{left.toTopCat}\), where
240	  \(J = \texttt{Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat}\) is the
241	  standard topology on the topological space underlying \(C\). The
242	  closed-point specialisation \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) is
243	  REF of
244	  REF; their agreement at \(D = [P]\) is
245	  REF below. The construction follows
246	  the iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom} recipe in
247	  \texttt{analogies/}: presheaf-by-hand via per-open
248	  \(\bar k\)-submodules of the function field, sheaf property reduced to
249	  the locality of the order conditions at the stalks of the prime
250	  divisors. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
251	\end{definition}
252	
253	\section{Immediate corollaries}
254	
255	\begin{lemma}\leanok
256	[Sheaf of the zero divisor is the structure sheaf]
257	  \label{lem:sheafOf_zero}
258	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_zero}
259	  \uses{def:sheafOf}
260	
261	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
262	  \(\bar k\). Then the invertible sheaf associated to the zero divisor
263	  \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is canonically the structure sheaf:
264	  \[
265	    \mathcal O_C(0) \;=\; \mathcal O_C
266	  \]
267	  as sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\), equivalently as
268	  objects in \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
269	\end{lemma}
270	
271	\begin{proof}
272	  
273	  At \(D = 0\) the coefficient \(n_Q = 0\) at every prime divisor \(Q\), so the
274	  section condition of REF reduces, on every open
275	  \(U \subseteq C\), to
276	  \[
277	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(0)\bigr)
278	    \;=\; \{ 0 \} \cup \{ f \in K(C)^{\times} \;|\;
279	      \ord_Q(f) \geq 0 \text{ for every prime divisor } Q \in U \}.
280	  \]
281	  By the local description of the structure sheaf on the integral
282	  scheme \(C\) (the regular functions on \(U\) are exactly the rational
283	  functions whose order is non-negative at every prime divisor of
284	  \(C\) landing in \(U\); this is the standard ``non-negative order \(=\)
285	  regular'' identification, e.g.\ Hartshorne~II~\S6 immediately before
286	  Proposition~6.11), the right-hand side is precisely
287	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)\). The restriction maps on both sides are
288	  the identity on \(K(C)\), so the equality of presheaves promotes to an
289	  equality of sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\).
290	\end{proof}
291	
292	\begin{lemma}\leanok
293	[Sheaf at a single closed point is the line bundle of
294	REF]
295	  \label{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint}
296	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_singlePoint}
297	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:divisor_closed_point, def:prime_divisor,
298	        def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
299	
300	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
301	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point, viewed as a Weil divisor
302	  \([P] \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) via REF. Then the
303	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) of REF agrees on
304	  the nose with the line bundle of the closed point
305	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) of REF:
306	  \[
307	    \mathcal O_C(D)\bigl|_{D = [P]}
308	    \;=\; \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}_{P}
309	  \]
310	  as sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\), equivalently as
311	  objects in \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
312	\end{lemma}
313	
314	\begin{proof}
315	  
316	  Unfold both definitions. On the \texttt{sheafOf} side, the formal sum
317	  \([P]\) has coefficients \(n_Q = 1\) if \(Q = P\) and \(n_Q = 0\) otherwise
318	  (where \(P\) is identified with the prime divisor it determines by
319	  REF). Thus
320	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) contains \(0\) and those
321	  \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) satisfying \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\) for every prime
322	  divisor \(Q \in U\) with \(Q \neq P\), plus \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\) if
323	  \(P \in U\).
324	
325	  On the REF side, the same conditions
326	  are exactly the ones extracted by
327	  REF of
328	  \texttt{RR.3}, at every open \(U\) (not only at the global section
329	  \(U = C\)): a rational function \(f \in K(C)\) is a section of
330	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) on \(U\) iff it lies in the structure sheaf at every
331	  \(Q \in U \setminus \{P\}\) and has at most a simple pole at \(P\) when
332	  \(P \in U\).
333	
334	  Restriction maps on both sides are the identity on \(K(C)\). The two
335	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\) are therefore equal
336	  section-by-section as subsets of \(K(C)\), and the equality propagates
337	  to an equality of sheaves of \(\mathcal O_C\)-modules. The
338	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on both sides is the restriction of the
339	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on \(K(C)\), so the identification is
340	  \(\bar k\)-linear on each section.
341	\end{proof}
342	
343	\begin{lemma}\leanok
344	[Short exact sequence for \(D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]\)]
345	  \label{lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add}
346	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_ses_single_add}
347	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:divisor_closed_point, def:prime_divisor}
348	
349	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive step of the proof
350	  % of Theorem~1.3, ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any
351	  % point...''). Read from
352	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313,
353	  % transcribed character-by-character from the rendered scanned page
354	  % image (the PDF has no text layer).
355	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
356	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
357	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
358	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
359	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
360	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
361	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\).
362	  % (Since \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by
363	  % it does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\).)"
364	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
365	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
366	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
367	  \(\bar k\), let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor, and let \(P \in C\) be
368	  a closed point (equivalently, a prime divisor
369	  REF on a smooth curve). Then the inclusion
370	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) of
371	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\) fits into a short exact
372	  sequence
373	  \[
374	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
375	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
376	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
377	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
378	  \]
379	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\) (equivalently, in
380	  \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\)), where \(k(P)\) denotes the
381	  skyscraper sheaf at \(P\) with stalk \(\bar k\) (the residue field of \(P\)
382	  is \(\bar k\) since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and \(P\) is a
383	  \(\bar k\)-rational closed point).
384	\end{lemma}
385	
386	\begin{proof}
387	  
388	  Hartshorne's argument (IV.1.3, the inductive step quoted above)
389	  proceeds in two beats:
390	
391	  \emph{Beat 1 (the ideal-sheaf SES at \(P\)).} The closed-subscheme
392	  inclusion \(P \hookrightarrow C\) has structure sheaf
393	  \(\mathcal O_P \cong k(P)\) (a skyscraper at \(P\) with stalk \(\bar k\)),
394	  and Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.18 identifies its ideal sheaf as
395	  \(\mathcal I_P = \mathcal O_C(-[P])\) (the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
396	  \(\mathcal K_C\) generated by a local uniformiser \(\pi_P\) at \(P\) and by
397	  \(1\) away from \(P\), i.e.\ the locally-principal ideal of the locally
398	  principal closed subscheme \(P\)). This produces the structural short
399	  exact sequence
400	  \[
401	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P])
402	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C
403	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
404	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
405	  \]
406	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\).
407	
408	  \emph{Beat 2 (tensor with \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\)).} The line bundle
409	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is locally free of rank~\(1\)
410	  (Hartshorne~II.6.13(a), REF), so $- \otimes_{\mathcal O_C}
411	  \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ is exact on $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$ and preserves
412	  the short exact sequence of Beat~1:
413	  \[
414	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes
415	      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
416	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
417	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
418	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0.
419	  \]
420	  Three identifications collapse this to the claimed sequence:
421	  \begin{itemize}
422	    \item $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
423	      \mathcal O_C(D)$ (tensor product of line bundles is the
424	      \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\)-additive operation $D' + D'' \mapsto \mathcal O_C(D' +
425	      D'')$ via Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(b), which is in turn the
426	      ring-theoretic identity
427	      $\pi_P^{-1} \cdot \pi_P^{1 + n_P^{D}} \cdot \prod_{Q \neq P}
428	      \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}} = \prod_Q \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}}$ on the generating
429	      rational functions, where \(n_Q^{D}\) is the coefficient of \(Q\)
430	      in \(D\));
431	    \item $\mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
432	      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ (the structure sheaf is the tensor unit);
433	    \item \(k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong k(P)\) (tensoring a
434	      skyscraper at \(P\) by a locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf preserves the
435	      skyscraper; concretely, at the stalk over \(P\) the line bundle
436	      \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])_P\) is a free \(\mathcal O_{C,P}\)-module of
437	      rank one, and $k(P) \otimes_{\mathcal O_{C,P}} \mathcal O_{C,P}
438	      \cong k(P)$ is just $k(P)$; away from $P$ the skyscraper is the
439	      zero sheaf, on which the tensor is the zero sheaf).
440	  \end{itemize}
441	  Substituting yields
442	  \[
443	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
444	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
445	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
446	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
447	  \]
448	  exact in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\), as required.
449	
450	  \emph{Compatibility with the structural inclusion.} The first map
451	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is the
452	  inclusion of subsheaves of \(\mathcal K_C\): at the level of sections,
453	  the constraint \(\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}\) of
454	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) implies the weaker constraint
455	  \(\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D} - 1\) of \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))\)
456	  when \(P \in U\) (constraints on \(Q \neq P\) are unchanged), and the map
457	  is identity on \(K(C)\) at the carrier level. The second map
458	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \twoheadrightarrow k(P)\) is the evaluation
459	  ``leading coefficient at the pole at \(P\)'' (i.e.\ the
460	  \(\pi_P^{-(n_P^{D} + 1)}\)-coefficient in the Laurent expansion of \(f\)
461	  at \(P\)), which is well-defined on \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))\)
462	  modulo the subgroup of sections with \(\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}\) ---
463	  i.e.\ modulo \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\).
464	\end{proof}
465	
466	\section{Sheaf-property correctness}
467	
468	The construction of REF is presheaf-by-hand: a
469	per-open \(\bar k\)-submodule of \(K(C)\), with restriction along
470	\(V \hookrightarrow U\) given by the identity on \(K(C)\). This section
471	sketches the verifications that make the construction land in the
472	target category \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
473	
474	\paragraph{Functoriality of the carrier.} For \(V \subseteq U\), the
475	constraint set defining \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) is a \emph{subset}
476	of the constraint set defining \(\Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))\) (fewer
477	prime divisors to constrain at), so the identity on \(K(C)\) restricts
478	to a well-defined inclusion
479	\(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D)) \hookrightarrow \Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))\).
480	Composition \(W \subseteq V \subseteq U\) is again identity-on-\(K(C)\), so
481	strict functoriality holds.
482	
483	\paragraph{\(\bar k\)-linearity.} A \(\bar k\)-scalar \(\lambda \in \bar k\)
484	acts on \(K(C)\) by multiplication, and
485	\(\ord_Q(\lambda \cdot f) = \ord_Q(f)\) for \(\lambda \neq 0\)
486	(units have order zero); for \(\lambda = 0\), \(\lambda \cdot f = 0\) is
487	included by convention. Thus the constraint set is a
488	\(\bar k\)-submodule and the restriction maps are \(\bar k\)-linear.
489	
490	\paragraph{Sheaf property.} The order condition
491	``\(\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q\) for every prime divisor \(Q \in U\)'' is
492	\emph{stalk-local} at each \(Q\): it depends only on the germ of \(f\) at
493	the point of \(C\) underlying \(Q\), equivalently on the image of \(f\) in
494	the DVR \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}\). Consequently the sheaf property reduces
495	to two ingredients:
496	\begin{enumerate}
497	  \item \emph{The presheaf-of-sets underlying \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is a
498	    subpresheaf of the constant presheaf at \(K(C)\).} The constant
499	    presheaf at \(K(C)\) is a sheaf (every locally-constant section of a
500	    constant presheaf on a topological space is globally constant when
501	    the space is irreducible, which the topological space underlying an
502	    integral scheme is). A subpresheaf of a sheaf is itself a sheaf iff
503	    its membership condition is local; the order conditions are local
504	    at each prime divisor's underlying point, so the membership
505	    condition is local across an open cover and a section glued from
506	    locally-defined sections inherits the order condition at every
507	    prime divisor.
508	  \item \emph{The forget-functor bridge from
509	    \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\) to
510	    \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Set})\).} The categorical forget
511	    \(\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k} \to \mathbf{Set}\) creates limits, hence
512	    reflects the sheaf property: a presheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules is a
513	    sheaf iff its underlying presheaf of sets is. Combined with~(i),
514	    this delivers the sheaf property for the
515	    \(\bar k\)-module-valued \(\mathcal O_C(D)\).
516	\end{enumerate}
517	
518	\paragraph{Invertibility.} On any open \(U \subseteq C\) disjoint from
519	the support of \(D\), the constraint set is exactly the structure-sheaf
520	constraint \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\) for every prime divisor \(Q \in U\); hence
521	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U = \mathcal O_C|_U\), and the trivialisation
522	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \cong \mathcal O_C|_U\) is the identity. On a small
523	open \(U \subseteq C\) containing exactly one prime divisor \(Q\) from the
524	support of \(D\), the local generator \(\pi_Q^{-n_Q} \in K(C)\) trivialises
525	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U\) as a free rank-\(1\) \(\mathcal O_C|_U\)-module: a
526	section \(f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) satisfies $\ord_Q(f) \geq
527	-n_Q$ iff $\pi_Q^{n_Q} \cdot f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)$, and the
528	``multiplication by \(\pi_Q^{n_Q}\)'' map is an
529	\(\mathcal O_C|_U\)-linear isomorphism
530	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal O_C|_U\). The two types
531	of trivialising opens cover \(C\) (any prime divisor lies in at most
532	finitely many supports across a covering, and the support of \(D\) is
533	finite); patching the trivialisations on overlaps amounts to changing
534	uniformiser, which is unit-times the original (by the DVR property), so
535	the cocycle is in \(\mathcal O_C^{\times}\) and the sheaf is invertible.
536	
537	The closure of (i)+(ii) in Lean follows the project's existing
538	\texttt{Scheme.toModuleKPresheaf} /
539	\texttt{toModuleKPresheaf\_isSheaf} template in
540	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/}, which
541	landed the analogous bottom-up
542	\(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\)-valued construction for the
543	structure sheaf. The order-condition locality is closed via the project's
544	\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} stalk bridge of
545	\texttt{RR.1} (REF and surrounding lemmas).
546	
547	\section{Lean signature pins (iter-183 file skeleton)}
548	
549	The Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean} (to be
550	created in iter-183 as a file-skeleton lane) will host exactly the
551	four pinned declarations of this chapter:
552	
553	\begin{itemize}
554	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf}
555	    (REF) — the invertible sheaf functor itself, replacing
556	    the typed-\texttt{sorry} body currently in
557	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean:168}. The
558	    \texttt{noncomputable def} signature is move-only across files;
559	    the body lives in \texttt{OcOfD.lean}, the
560	    \texttt{RRFormula.lean} entry becomes a re-export (or the signature
561	    itself moves wholesale and \texttt{RRFormula.lean}'s consumers
562	    re-import).
563	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf\_zero}
564	    (REF) — the structure-sheaf identification at
565	    \(D = 0\).
566	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf\_singlePoint}
567	    (REF) — the
568	    REF comparison at \(D = [P]\),
569	    upstream of REF's
570	    consumption in \texttt{RR.3}.
571	  \item
572	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf\_ses\_single\_add}
573	    (REF) — the inductive-step short
574	    exact sequence consumed by the two helper sorries
575	    \texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_zero} and
576	    \texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_succ} of \texttt{RR.2}.
577	\end{itemize}
578	
579	The iter-183 file-skeleton lane will create these four declarations as
580	typed sorries; the iter-184+ prover lanes will close the bodies along
581	the recipe sketched in \
582	(via the project's existing \texttt{toModuleKPresheaf} template) and
583	unblock both \texttt{RR.2}'s helper sorries and the \texttt{RR.3}
584	\(\mathcal O_C([P])\) identification.
585	
586	\section{Out of scope}
587	
588	\begin{itemize}
589	  \item \textbf{Linear-equivalence isomorphism.} The natural
590	    isomorphism \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when
591	    \(D \sim D'\) (i.e.\ \(D - D' = \div(f)\) for some
592	    \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)) is \emph{not} formalised in this chapter.
593	    It is not needed for the genus-\(0\) critical path: \texttt{RR.2}
594	    consumes only \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D))\) and the SES additivity at
595	    closed points; \texttt{RR.3} consumes only the closed-point
596	    specialisation; \texttt{RR.4} consumes the function-field /
597	    degree bridge, which is divisorial in \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) rather than
598	    in \(\mathrm{Pic}(C) = \mathrm{Div}(C) / \sim\). The
599	    Cartier-divisor \(\leftrightarrow\) \texttt{Pic} isomorphism is
600	    queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter.
601	  \item \textbf{Sheaf-multiplication
602	    $\mathcal O_C(D) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D') \cong \mathcal O_C(D +
603	    D')$.} The tensor-product identity of Hartshorne's
604	    Proposition~6.13(b) is invoked inside the proof of
605	    REF at the single instance
606	    $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
607	    \mathcal O_C(D)$, but the \emph{statement} of
608	    Proposition~6.13(b) as a separate lemma is not formalised in this
609	    chapter. The instance used inside the SES proof is local to that
610	    proof; a generalised tensor-additivity lemma is queued for the
611	    post-RR-bridge follow-up.
612	  \item \textbf{Pullback of \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) along a morphism.} The
613	    pullback \(f^* \mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_{C'}(f^* D)\) for
614	    a morphism of curves \(f : C' \to C\), used in degree-computation
615	    arguments on a base change, is \emph{not} formalised in this
616	    chapter and is not needed for the genus-\(0\) critical path.
617	  \item \textbf{Global-sections identification as the Riemann--Roch
618	    space.} The identification
619	    \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \cong L(D)\) for a general Weil divisor
620	    \(D\) (i.e.\ the analogue of
621	    REF for general
622	    \(D\)) is queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up. The closed-point
623	    case \(D = [P]\) is the only specialisation consumed by the
624	    genus-\(0\) critical path, and it is handled in
625	    \texttt{RR.3} via REF.
626	  \item \textbf{Serre duality and the canonical sheaf.} As recorded in
627	    the \texttt{RR.2} and \texttt{RR.3} chapters, Mathlib (snapshot
628	    \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme
629	    and no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) critical path
630	    does \emph{not} require either; the present chapter accordingly
631	    does not engage them.
632	\end{itemize}
633

3	\chapter{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero (RR.2)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, IV \S1, pp.~294--297
7	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages
8	% 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below
9	% were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of
10	% the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter is the second of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
15	that close the project's headline RR bridge
16	\[
17	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
18	  \,\colon\,
19	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
20	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
21	\]
22	The bridge consumed by
23	REF of
24	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} is the Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain:
25	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) and the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
26	\to \mathbb Z$ (\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the
27	Riemann--Roch dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
28	\[
29	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
30	\]
31	(this chapter, \texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections identification
32	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) (\texttt{RR.3}); the
33	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
34	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
35	
36	% NOTE (Serre duality vs. direct \(\chi\) — iter-173 sub-phase choice).
37	% This chapter proves the RR formula via the \emph{direct \(\chi\)
38	% inductive proof} (Hartshorne IV.1.3 step ``Next, let \(D\) be any
39	% divisor\ldots'', p.~296), \emph{not} via Serre duality. The reason is
40	% strictly Mathlib-side: Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships no
41	% dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and no Serre duality theorem
42	% \(H^i(X, \mathcal F) \cong H^{n-i}(X, \omega_X \otimes \mathcal F^\vee)^*\);
43	% routing through that machinery would force a multi-iter sub-build
44	% strictly heavier than the genus-\(0\) critical path requires. The
45	% direct \(\chi\) proof depends only on additivity of the Euler
46	% characteristic on a short exact sequence of coherent sheaves (a
47	% standard preadditive-category fact), the genus base case
48	% \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\), and the closed-point skyscraper
49	% identity \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).
50	
51	% NOTE (Genus-\(0\) carve-out vs. general \(g\) — iter-173 sub-phase
52	% choice). The chapter restricts the headline theorem
53	% REF to the genus-\(0\) specialisation
54	% \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). The general-\(g\)
55	% statement \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) is downstream of
56	% \texttt{RR.2} (it requires the canonical divisor \(K\) and Serre
57	% duality / the \(K - D\) pairing) and is \emph{not} needed for the
58	% genus-\(0\) critical path: \texttt{RR.3} consumes only
59	% REF, and \texttt{RR.4} consumes
60	% \texttt{RR.3}. The intermediate \(\chi\)-identity
61	% REF is stated and
62	% sketched for general \(g\) (the inductive argument is genus-uniform);
63	% its full closure on a general-\(g\) curve waits for the project's
64	% subsequent Riemann--Roch chapter, but the proof body in
65	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} pins the
66	% \emph{statement} for general \(g\) and may close the body initially
67	% under a \(g = 0\) hypothesis.
68	
69	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
70	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
71	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
72	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}) carry the same
73	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
74	\begin{quote}
75	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
76	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
77	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
78	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
79	\end{quote}
80	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
81	\texttt{Genus.tex}, namely \(g(C) = \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\)
82	via the project's \(\Module\bar k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology
83	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}).
84	
85	\section{The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve}
86	
87	\begin{definition}
88	
89	
90	\leanok
91	[Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve]
92	  \label{def:eulerChar_curve}
93	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic}
94	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule}
95	
96	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(\chi(\mathcal F)\)
97	  % inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
98	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
99	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
100	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
101	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
102	  % is the Euler characteristic
103	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
104	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295, the formula displayed
105	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3.}
106	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F\)
107	  be a coherent sheaf on \(C\). The \emph{Euler characteristic} of
108	  \(\mathcal F\) is the integer
109	  \[
110	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;:=\;
111	    \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \,\dim_{\bar k} H^i(C, \mathcal F)
112	    \;=\;
113	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal F)
114	    \,-\,
115	    \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal F).
116	  \]
117	  The second equality is a curve-specific simplification: because
118	  \(\dim C = 1\), Grothendieck vanishing (Hartshorne~III.2.7) gives
119	  \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\), so the alternating sum has
120	  only two terms. Coherence of \(\mathcal F\) on the proper $\bar
121	  k$-scheme $C$ guarantees that both $H^0$ and $H^1$ are
122	  finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology
123	  finiteness theorem, the same finiteness backing
124	  REF), so the two terms are honest natural numbers and
125	  the difference is a well-defined integer.
126	
127	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
128	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic} takes a coherent
129	  sheaf \(\mathcal F\) on \(C\) and returns an integer; the natural input
130	  shape is the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
131	  wrapper \(\HModule \bar k\) (REF) applied to a
132	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured carrier built from \(\mathcal F\), with
133	  \(\dim_{\bar k}\) extracted via \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\). The
134	  declaration is \texttt{noncomputable} (inherited from
135	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) and from the \(\Ext\)-based cohomology
136	  construction).
137	\end{definition}
138	
139	\section{The \(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor}
140	
141	\begin{definition}
142	
143	
144	\leanok
145	[\(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor]
146	  \label{def:l_invariant}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l}
148	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:codim1_cycles, def:sheafOf}
149	
150	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\) and of the
151	  % "dimension of \(|D|\)") (read from
152	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
153	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We denote \(\dim_k H^0(X, \mathscr L(D))\) by \(l(D)\),
154	  % so that the \emph{dimension of \(|D|\)} is \(l(D) - 1\). The number
155	  % \(l(D)\) is finite by (II, 5.19) or (III, 5.2)."
156	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\)).}
157	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let
158	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor (REF). The
159	  \emph{\(\ell\)-invariant of \(D\)} is the integer
160	  \[
161	    \ell(D) \;:=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
162	    \;\in\; \mathbb N,
163	  \]
164	  the \(\bar k\)-dimension of the space of global sections of the
165	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\). Finiteness of
166	  \(\ell(D)\) is a consequence of the coherent-cohomology finiteness
167	  theorem on a proper \(\bar k\)-scheme (Hartshorne~II.5.19 or
168	  III.5.2). The classical \emph{complete linear system \(|D|\)} has
169	  \(\bar k\)-dimension \(\ell(D) - 1\) as a projective space, but the
170	  chapter never uses the projective-space interpretation; \(\ell(D)\) is
171	  the only quantity it needs.
172	
173	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
174	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l} accepts a divisor
175	  \(D : C.\mathtt{WeilDivisor}\) (REF) and returns
176	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\;(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)))\),
177	  computed through the same \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
178	  pipeline as REF. The associated invertible
179	  sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is the line bundle of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
180	  chapter, to be added); the present chapter's prose and Lean
181	  signature reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) by name and consume only its
182	  \(H^0\) functor. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
183	\end{definition}
184	
185	\section{The \(\chi\) identity: \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\)}
186	
187	\subsection*{Substrate lemmas for the proof of the \(\chi\)-identity}
188	
189	The proof of REF below
190	decomposes into three substantive substrate lemmas, factored out of the
191	Hartshorne~IV.1.3 inductive argument. The first
192	(REF, \emph{additivity}) is the
193	classical statement that \(\chi\) descends to the Grothendieck group
194	of \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\); the second
195	(REF, \emph{iso-invariance}) is the
196	elementary fact that \(\chi\) is well defined on isomorphism classes
197	of sheaves; the third
198	(REF, \emph{skyscraper}) computes \(\chi\)
199	on a closed-point skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\). Pinning each as its own
200	Lean declaration lets the prover attack the three substrate gaps
201	independently of the parent inductive argument.
202	
203	\begin{lemma}\leanok
204	[Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence]
205	  \label{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add}
206	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add}
207	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:Scheme_HModule}
208	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (inside the proof of Theorem~1.3,
209	  % the citation ``III, Ex.~5.1'' for the additivity of \(\chi\) on a
210	  % short exact sequence) (read from
211	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
212	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
213	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
214	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
215	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (citation of III, Ex.~5.1
216	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
217	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
218	  \(\bar k\), and let
219	  \[
220	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
221	  \]
222	  be a short exact sequence in the category of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
223	  sheaves on \(C\). Then
224	  \[
225	    \chi(\mathcal F_2) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3).
226	  \]
227	\end{lemma}
228	
229	\begin{proof}
230	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
231	  Apply the project-side \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured covariant Ext-LES
232	  (Mathlib's \texttt{Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence}) to the constant-sheaf
233	  test object \(L = (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
234	  \bar k\;\bar k)\) and the short exact sequence
235	  \(0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0\). The
236	  resulting nine-term sequence
237	  \[
238	    H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to H^0(\mathcal F_3)
239	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2) \to H^1(\mathcal F_3)
240	    \to H^2(\mathcal F_1) \to H^2(\mathcal F_2) \to H^2(\mathcal F_3)
241	  \]
242	  is exact in the category of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces. Grothendieck
243	  vanishing \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\) on the
244	  one-dimensional scheme \(C\) (Hartshorne~III.2.7) truncates the
245	  sequence to a six-term exact sequence
246	  \[
247	    0 \to K_0 \to H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to
248	    H^0(\mathcal F_3) \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2)
249	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_3) \to 0
250	  \]
251	  (with \(K_0 = 0\) the kernel of the first map, kept here only as a
252	  bookkeeping placeholder). Rank-counting on a six-term exact sequence
253	  of finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces — iterated applications
254	  of \(\mathtt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank}\) at each
255	  intermediate kernel/image quotient — yields the alternating identity
256	  \[
257	    \dim H^0(\mathcal F_1) - \dim H^0(\mathcal F_2) + \dim H^0(\mathcal F_3)
258	    - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_1) + \dim H^1(\mathcal F_2) - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_3)
259	    \;=\; 0,
260	  \]
261	  which rearranges, using the definition
262	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
263	  (REF), to
264	  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3)\).
265	
266	  % NOTE: gated on project-side LES carrier + finiteness machinery.
267	  % Mathlib `b80f227` ships `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` but not (a)
268	  % the `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured LES carrier specialised to the
269	  % project's sheaf-cohomology pipeline `Scheme.HModule`, (b) Grothendieck
270	  % vanishing `H^i(C, F) = 0` for `i ≥ 2` at the `Ext`-cohomology level
271	  % consumed by `HModule`, nor (c) the six-term rank-counting bridge
272	  % composed of `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank` invocations.
273	  % The Lean helper `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` therefore
274	  % carries a Tier-3 honest typed sorry; its closure lands once the
275	  % three substrate pieces are in place. Off the genus-0 critical path
276	  % (the parent theorem `thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`
277	  % is the only consumer in `RR.2`).
278	\end{proof}
279	
280	\begin{lemma}\leanok
281	[Iso-invariance of the Euler characteristic]
282	  \label{lem:euler_char_iso}
283	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_iso}
284	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
285	  \textit{Project-bespoke decomposition} (Archon-original packaging of
286	  the Mathlib \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; no external source named).
287	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
288	  \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F, \mathcal G\) be \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
289	  sheaves on \(C\) with an isomorphism \(\mathcal F \cong \mathcal G\).
290	  Then
291	  \[
292	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal G).
293	  \]
294	\end{lemma}
295	
296	\begin{proof}
297	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
298	  \leanok
299	  An isomorphism \(e \colon \mathcal F \xrightarrow{\;\cong\;} \mathcal G\)
300	  induces, in each cohomological degree \(n \in \mathbb N\), a
301	  \(\bar k\)-linear equivalence
302	  \[
303	    H^n(C, \mathcal F) \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\; H^n(C, \mathcal G)
304	  \]
305	  by post-composition with the Ext-degree-zero classes
306	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{hom})\) and
307	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{inv})\). The mutual-inverse identities
308	  for this pair of post-compositions reduce — via the associativity
309	  lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.comp\_assoc\_of\_third\_deg\_zero}\), the
310	  composition lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0\mathtt{\_comp\_mk}_0\), and the
311	  iso identities \(e.\mathtt{hom} \circ e.\mathtt{inv} = \mathrm{id}\),
312	  \(e.\mathtt{inv} \circ e.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}\) — to the identity
313	  map on \(\Ext\) elements. Specialising to \(n = 0\) and \(n = 1\) and
314	  applying \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) at each of those two
315	  degrees gives
316	  \(\dim H^n(C, \mathcal F) = \dim H^n(C, \mathcal G)\) for
317	  \(n \in \{0, 1\}\). Unfolding the definition
318	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
319	  (REF) closes the goal.
320	\end{proof}

369	\begin{lemma}\leanok
370	[Euler characteristic of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf]
371	  \label{lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
372	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_skyscraperSheaf}
373	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve,
374	        lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one,
375	        lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
376	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) input
377	  % cited inline inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
378	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
379	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
380	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
381	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
382	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (the ``\(\chi(k(P)) = 1\)''
383	  input inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
384	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
385	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Write \(k(P)\) for
386	  the closed-point skyscraper sheaf
387	  \[
388	    k(P) \;:=\; \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
389	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k),
390	  \]
391	  the \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf on \(C\) with stalk \(\bar k\) at
392	  \(P\) and zero stalks elsewhere. Then
393	  \[
394	    \chi(k(P)) \;=\; 1.
395	  \]
396	\end{lemma}
397	
398	\begin{proof}
399	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve,
400	        lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one,
401	        lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
402	  \leanok
403	  By the definition of \(\chi\)
404	  (REF),
405	  \[
406	    \chi(k(P)) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P))
407	    \;-\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)).
408	  \]
409	  The closure decomposes cleanly into two independent half-statements
410	  (\cref{lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one} and
411	  \cref{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}), attacked separately
412	  by the prover.
413	
414	  \emph{H\(^0\) half} — \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\).
415	  Compose the project's three-step \(\bar k\)-linear-equivalence chain
416	  \[
417	    H^0(C, k(P))
418	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
419	    \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Sh}}\!\bigl(
420	      (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k),\,
421	      k(P)
422	    \bigr)
423	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
424	    \Gamma(C, k(P))
425	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
426	    (k(P))(C),
427	  \]
428	  realised by the project lemmas
429	  \texttt{Scheme.HModule\_zero\_linearEquiv},
430	  \texttt{Scheme.constantSheafGammaHom\_linearEquiv}, and
431	  \texttt{Scheme.SheafGammaObj\_linearEquiv\_top} (the last step
432	  identifies the abstract \(\Gamma\) functor with the underlying
433	  presheaf evaluation at the top open \(\top\), i.e.\ at the whole
434	  underlying space of \(C\)). Applying
435	  \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) along this chain reduces the
436	  goal to
437	  \(\dim_{\bar k} (k(P))(C) = 1\). The closed point
438	  \(P\) lies in the top open of \(C\), so the skyscraper presheaf
439	  evaluates at the top open to its stalk
440	  \(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k\) by definition, and
441	  \(\dim_{\bar k} \bar k = 1\) closes the half. This half is
442	  axiom-clean closure projection (\(\sim 30\text{--}60\) LOC) against
443	  the existing project chain; it is the natural target for the
444	  iteration that follows the present blueprint expansion.
445	
446	  \emph{H\(^1\) half} — \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
447	  Mathematically, the closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque (any
448	  pushforward of a sheaf from a one-point space is flasque;
449	  Hartshorne~III.2.5(c)), and flasque sheaves have vanishing higher
450	  cohomology in degrees \(\geq 1\) on any topological space
451	  (Hartshorne~III.2.5). Hence \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) as a
452	  \(\bar k\)-vector space.
453	
454	  % NOTE: gated on Mathlib flasque cohomology.
455	  % Mathlib `b80f227` does NOT ship an `H^n(X, F) = 0 for flasque F`
456	  % lemma at the `ModuleCat`-flavoured `Abelian.Ext`-cohomology level
457	  % used by the project's `Scheme.HModule kbar` pipeline. The closure
458	  % depends on either (a) an upstream Mathlib
459	  % `Sheaf.flasque_vanishing_cohomology` contribution at the
460	  % `Ext`-cohomology level for sheaves with values in an abelian
461	  % category with enough injectives, or (b) a project-side flasque-
462	  % cohomology bridge (~150-300 LOC, off the genus-0 critical path).
463	  % Until one of the two lands, the H¹ half is a typed-sorry blocker
464	  % independent of the H⁰ half — i.e. the prover can attack the H⁰
465	  % half against the existing project chain without waiting for the
466	  % H¹ machinery, and the present skyscraper lemma waits for the H¹
467	  % gap to clear.
468	
469	  Combining the two halves through the definition of \(\chi\),
470	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1 - 0 = 1\), closes the lemma.
471	\end{proof}

473	\begin{lemma}[Global sections of the structure sheaf are one-dimensional]
474	  \label{lem:finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one}
475	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one}
476	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:Scheme_toModuleKSheaf}
477	  \textit{Specialisation of Hartshorne~I.3.4 (``\(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X) = k\)
478	  for any projective variety over an algebraically closed field'') to the
479	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline.}
480	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
481	  \(\bar k\). The zeroth cohomology of the structure-sheaf carrier
482	  \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is one-dimensional over \(\bar k\):
483	  \[
484	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
485	    \;=\; \dim_{\bar k}\, \HModule\,\bar k\,(\toModuleKSheaf C)\,0
486	    \;=\; 1.
487	  \]
488	  The proof identifies \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)\) with the global-sections
489	  ring \(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \mathcal O_C(\top)\) through the
490	  project's \(\bar k\)-linear-equivalence chain
491	  (\cref{def:Scheme_HModule}). On the proper \(\bar k\)-scheme \(C\) this
492	  ring is a finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-algebra; integrality of \(C\)
493	  makes it a domain; and a finite \(\bar k\)-algebra that is an integral
494	  domain over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) is \(\bar k\)
495	  itself, so its \(\bar k\)-dimension is \(1\).
496	\end{lemma}
497	
498	\begin{proof}
499	  Proved directly in Lean.
500	\end{proof}

502	\begin{lemma}[\(\chi\)-additivity across a short exact sequence with skyscraper cokernel]
503	  \label{lem:euler_char_of_shortExact_skyscraper}
504	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_of_shortExact_skyscraper}
505	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, lem:euler_char_shortExact_add,
506	        lem:euler_char_iso, lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
507	  \textit{Project-bespoke packaging of the Hartshorne~IV.1.3 inductive
508	  step, combining the three substrate lemmas above.}
509	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
510	  \(\bar k\), let
511	  \[
512	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
513	  \]
514	  be a short exact sequence of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaves on
515	  \(C\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point such that the cokernel
516	  term \(\mathcal F_3\) is isomorphic to the skyscraper sheaf
517	  \(k(P) = \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\).
518	  Then
519	  \[
520	    \chi(\mathcal F_2) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal F_1) + 1.
521	  \]
522	\end{lemma}
523	
524	\begin{proof}
525	  \uses{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add, lem:euler_char_iso,
526	        lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
527	  Additivity of the Euler characteristic across the short exact sequence
528	  (\cref{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add}) gives
529	  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3)\).
530	  Iso-invariance of \(\chi\) (\cref{lem:euler_char_iso}) applied to the
531	  isomorphism \(\mathcal F_3 \cong k(P)\) rewrites the last term as
532	  \(\chi(k(P))\), and the skyscraper computation
533	  (\cref{lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}) evaluates \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).
534	  Combining the three identities yields
535	  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + 1\).
536	\end{proof}

538	\subsection*{Inductive scaffolding via the divisor line bundle}
539	
540	The base case and the single-point inductive step of the
541	\(\chi\)-identity \cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} are
542	pinned as three named Lean lemmas. Each references the line bundle
543	\(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of \texttt{RR.3} (\cref{def:sheafOf}) and so carries a
544	typed-sorry transitivity inherited from that sibling chapter; the
545	arithmetic scaffolding around \(\chi\) is, however, honest.
546	
547	\begin{lemma}[Base case of the \(\chi\)-identity]
548	  \label{lem:euler_char_sheafOf_zero}
549	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_zero}
550	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, lem:finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one,
551	        lem:sheafOf_zero, def:genus}
552	  \textit{Base case of Hartshorne~IV.1.3 (the \(D = 0\) step).}
553	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
554	  \(\bar k\). The Euler characteristic of the line bundle
555	  \(\mathcal O_C(0)\) of the zero divisor is
556	  \[
557	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(0)) \;=\; 1 - g(C).
558	  \]
559	\end{lemma}
560	
561	\begin{proof}
562	  \uses{lem:finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one, lem:sheafOf_zero, def:genus}
563	  The line bundle of the zero divisor is the structure-sheaf carrier,
564	  \(\mathcal O_C(0) = \toModuleKSheaf C\) (\cref{lem:sheafOf_zero}).
565	  Unfolding the two-term Euler characteristic
566	  \(\chi = \dim_{\bar k} H^0 - \dim_{\bar k} H^1\)
567	  (\cref{def:eulerChar_curve}) reduces the claim to
568	  \[
569	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
570	    - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \;=\; 1 - g(C).
571	  \]
572	  The first term equals \(1\)
573	  (\cref{lem:finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one}), and the second term is by
574	  definition the genus, \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g(C)\)
575	  (\cref{def:genus}). Hence \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(0)) = 1 - g(C)\).
576	\end{proof}
577	
578	\begin{lemma}[Unit inductive step of the \(\chi\)-identity]
579	  \label{lem:euler_char_sheafOf_succ}
580	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_succ}
581	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, lem:euler_char_of_shortExact_skyscraper,
582	        lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add, def:sheafOf}
583	  \textit{Single-point inductive step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3 (the
584	  ``\(D \leftrightsquigarrow D + P\)'' step, additive direction).}
585	  Let \(C\) be as above, let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\), and let \(Y \in C\)
586	  be a prime (closed-point) divisor. Adding the single point \(Y\) raises
587	  the Euler characteristic by one:
588	  \[
589	    \chi(\mathcal O_C([Y] + D)) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1.
590	  \]
591	\end{lemma}
592	
593	\begin{proof}
594	  \uses{lem:euler_char_of_shortExact_skyscraper, lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add}
595	  The closed-point short exact sequence of \texttt{RR.3}
596	  (\cref{lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add}) provides a short exact sequence
597	  \[
598	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(D) \to \mathcal O_C([Y] + D) \to k(Y) \to 0
599	  \]
600	  of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaves whose cokernel is the closed-point
601	  skyscraper \(k(Y)\). Applying the packaged \(\chi\)-additivity with
602	  skyscraper cokernel (\cref{lem:euler_char_of_shortExact_skyscraper}) to
603	  this sequence yields
604	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C([Y] + D)) = \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1\).
605	\end{proof}
606	
607	\begin{lemma}[General single-point inductive step of the \(\chi\)-identity]
608	  \label{lem:euler_char_sheafOf_single_add}
609	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_single_add}
610	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, lem:euler_char_sheafOf_succ, def:sheafOf}
611	  \textit{Iterated single-point step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3, both signs of
612	  the multiplicity.}
613	  Let \(C\) be as above, let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\), let \(Y \in C\) be
614	  a prime divisor, and let \(n \in \mathbb Z\). Then
615	  \[
616	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(n[Y] + D)) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + n.
617	  \]
618	\end{lemma}
619	
620	\begin{proof}
621	  \uses{lem:euler_char_sheafOf_succ}
622	  Induction on \(n \in \mathbb Z\). For \(n = 0\) the divisor \(n[Y]\)
623	  vanishes and the identity is trivial. For the positive direction,
624	  \((n+1)[Y] + D = [Y] + (n[Y] + D)\), so the unit step
625	  (\cref{lem:euler_char_sheafOf_succ}) and the inductive hypothesis give
626	  \[
627	    \chi(\mathcal O_C((n+1)[Y] + D))
628	    \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(n[Y] + D)) + 1
629	    \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + (n+1).
630	  \]
631	  The negative direction runs the same unit step backwards: applying
632	  \cref{lem:euler_char_sheafOf_succ} to the divisor \((n-1)[Y] + D\) and
633	  rearranging gives
634	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C((n-1)[Y] + D)) = \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + (n-1)\),
635	  which is the inductive hypothesis transported one step in the negative
636	  direction.
637	\end{proof}
